original_abstract: On February 6, 2023, Türkiye experienced one of the most devastating earthquake disasters in its history, significantly impacting ten provinces. This study evaluates the comprehensive response of the Ministry of National Education (MoNE) in the aftermath of the disaster, focusing on its dual role as a provider of humanitarian aid and a facilitator of educational continuity. Guided by international frameworks for education in emergencies, such as those established by INEE and UNESCO-IIEP, the MoNE leveraged its human resources and the production capacity of vocational institutions to mitigate the disaster's effects. Findings indicate that the MoNE served as a primary producer and distributor of food, heating materials, and shelter for affected citizens. Simultaneously, the Ministry prioritized the rapid resumption of schooling by establishing learning environments in tents, containers, and prefabricated buildings, while deploying volunteer teachers and psychological counselors to provide psychosocial support. Key policy actions included the creation of hospital classrooms for injured students, the facilitation of school transfers to other provinces, and the provision of free boarding and private education scholarships for earthquake victims. The study concludes that the MoNE effectively integrated its previous experience from the COVID-19 pandemic into its disaster response, successfully implementing evidence-based policies to ensure that education remained uninterrupted despite the extensive regional destruction.
humanized_abstract: The 6 February 2023 Turkey–Syria earthquake occurred on 6 February 2023 at 04:17:32.1 (UTC) in Turkey and Syria, striking approximately 33 kilometres (21 miles) north-northwest of the city of Gaziantep, impacting ten provinces in Türkiye. This paper examines the overall response of the Ministry of National Education (MoNE) to the disaster, as a humanitarian organization and as an education provider. Applying international best practices defined by the INEE and UNESCO-IIEP, the MoNE made full use of the capacities it has as a huge organisation having all human resources and the production potential of vocational schools to mitigate the disaster effects. According to the research results, while the MoNE produced and distributed materials needed for food, heating and shelter for the people negatively affected in the earthquake, it also ensured that the education continued efficiently by establishing schools in tents, containers and modular buildings, by sending volunteer teachers, psychological counsellors to provide mental support to the affected students and by implementing other education-related measures as hospital classrooms for the students injured in the earthquake, to enable the students who are affected in the earthquake and have to move to other provinces to attend schools, to offer free accommodation to affected students who have to move to other cities and to provide monthly private education scholarships for affected students. As a result of the research, the MoNE implemented the measures based on the lessons learned from the COVID-19 period in a very effective way and implemented the education continuity proofs based on the evidence in the best possible way, considering the serious destruction in the earthquake affected region in Türkiye.
